critique.summary: The article provides a comprehensive cheat sheet for using Claude Code, detailing keyboard shortcuts, server management commands, slash commands, memory and file handling, workflows, configuration options, skills, agents, and CLI flags. It serves as a reference guide for users to efficiently operate the Claude Code environment.
mainPoints: Keyboard shortcuts for general controls, mode switching, input, and session navigation.
Commands for managing MCP servers, including adding servers and listing them.
Various slash commands for session management, configuration, tools, and special functions.
Details on memory and file locations, rules, and auto memory handling.
Workflows and tips including plan mode, thinking and effort toggles, Git worktrees, voice mode, and context management.
Configuration files and environment variables relevant to Claude Code.
Built-in skills and agents with customization options.
CLI commands and flags for interacting with Claude Code from the command line.
critique.truthfulness: The content appears to be a straightforward technical reference or cheat sheet for Claude Code, listing commands and features without making factual claims that require external verification. The information aligns with typical command-line interface documentation and developer tool references. There are no obvious inaccuracies or misleading statements, but the source is not a recognized journalistic outlet and does not cite external sources.
considerations: The article does not provide context about Claude Code's purpose, target audience, or how it compares to similar tools.
No information is given about the reliability, security, or privacy implications of using these commands and features.
There is no mention of the software's version or update history, which could affect command availability or behavior.
The article lacks examples or explanations for how to use the commands effectively in real scenarios.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a cheat sheet for Claude Code, with users sharing feedback on missing features, usability concerns, and suggestions for improvements. Some praise the resource's utility, while others question the need for such a cheat sheet or suggest alternative interfaces.

comments.author: phasE89
comments.text:
I use Claude Code daily but kept forgetting commands, so I had Claude research every feature from the docs and GitHub, then generate a printable A4 landscape HTML page covering keyboard shortcuts, slash commands, workflows, skills system, memory/CLAUDE.md, MCP setup, CLI flags, and config files.

It's a single HTML file - Claude wrote it and I iterated on the layout. A daily cron job checks the changelog and updates the sheet automatically, tagging new features with a "NEW" badge.

Auto-detects Mac/Windows for the right shortcuts. Shows current Claude Code version and a dismissable changelog of recent changes at the top.

It will always be lightweight, free, no signup required: https://cc.storyfox.cz

Ctrl+P to print. Works on mobile too.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and practical solution to the problem of forgetting commands, including an automated update mechanism, making it highly credible and useful.

comments.author: comboy
comments.text:
Wow /insights is genuinely useful, perhaps CLI should be pushing that as a tip, if one has enough sessions, instead of keep nagging me about the frontend developer skill which I already have installed

In general CLI could be more reliable and responsive though, it's a text based env yet sometimes feel like running windows 95 on 386dx

It seems clear from the insights that some model is marking failure cases when things went wrong and likely reporting home, so that should be extremely valuable to Anthropic
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully critiques the CLI's usability and suggests improvements, indicating a good understanding of the tool's behavior and user experience.

comments.author: bibimsz
comments.text: Thanks for putting this together! It's really nice to have a quick reference of all the features at a glance — especially since new features are being added all the time. Saves a lot of digging through docs.
comments.analysis: Expresses appreciation for the cheat sheet's utility, reinforcing its value for users needing quick access to features.

comments.author: kxrm
comments.text:
CMD + V to paste an image is wrong.

On Mac it's the same as Windows, CTRL + V.

You use CMD + V to paste text.
comments.analysis: Corrects a specific technical detail about keyboard shortcuts, providing accurate and helpful information.

comments.author: bingemaker
comments.text: Nice work. Under "MCP" section, "Local" shouldn't be prepended with "~". It should just be `.claude.json (per project)`
comments.analysis: Offers a precise correction regarding file path notation, improving the cheat sheet's accuracy.

comments.author: apoorvdarshan
comments.text: dangerously skip permission is all u need
comments.analysis: Emphasizes the importance of a specific CLI flag, though the comment is brief and somewhat informal.

comments.author: bobjordan
comments.text: Surprised that my most used flag `--dangerously-skip-permissions` is not on it
comments.analysis: Points out a notable omission in the cheat sheet, aligning with other comments referencing the same flag.

comments.author: vasco
comments.text: Just ask it, this is not needed
comments.analysis: Suggests that the cheat sheet may be redundant given the AI's capabilities, a valid perspective though lacking detail.

comments.author: droidjj
comments.text: The fact this needs to exist seems like a UX red flag.
comments.analysis: Critiques the user experience, implying the interface should be intuitive enough to not require a cheat sheet.

comments.author: artyom
comments.text:
Wait, why do we need chat sheets for this like it's (gasp!) a programming language, tool or IDE?

it's almost like if the thing is not intelligent at all and just another abstraction on top of what we already had.
comments.analysis: Questions the intelligence of the tool and the necessity of cheat sheets, offering a skeptical viewpoint.
